votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks if 'res' matches the boolean value of the condition that one integer is the sum of the other two, which is exactly equivalent to the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the boolean 'res' matches the truth value of the disjunction.
The Python code correctly checks if 'res' is equivalent to the disjunction of the three equality conditions, matching the Coq specification.

The Coq spec states res = true iff one of the three sum conditions holds. The Python code returns True iff res equals the boolean value of the disjunction of those same three conditions. For concrete inputs, when res is True/true, both require at least one condition to hold; when res is False/false, both require none to hold. The Python correctly implements this equivalence check.

The Python correctly captures this equivalence for all concrete integer inputs.

Returns True exactly when res equals (x==y+z or y==x+z or z==x+y); Python ints match Coq Z, so behavior aligns on all typed inputs.
Both enforce that res is true exactly when x=y+z or y=x+z or z=x+y; Python uses arbitrary-precision ints like Coq Z, so results match on all concrete inputs.
Both capture that res is true iff one of x=y+z, y=x+z, or z=x+y holds; the Python returns True exactly when res equals that condition.
